macro_segments.title: Introduction and Review of Bar Padda's Doctrine on Redemption and Re-sanctification

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and context from prior daf
Bar Padda's principle: items under neder cannot lose status unless redeemed, and status renews continuously
Application: saplings declared konam until cut down maintain status through ongoing renewal

macro_segments.title: Rav Oshaya's Query: Two Rings and Conditional Kiddushin

micro_segments.title: Rav Oshaya's question: man gives two coins to woman, first ring marries her now, second ring marries her after divorce
Proposed parallel to saplings: both are acts with conditional or delayed effect
Explanation of how one act can take effect now and be reinstated in future

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yirmiyah's Objection and the Distinction between Personal and Third-Party Redemption

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yirmiyah wakes and challenges the comparison; introduces Rabbi Yochanan's response
Rabbi Yochanan: if the owner redeems, status is reinstated; if third party redeems, status is lost
Explanation: control over the object determines whether original statement can take effect
Application to woman: she is like a third party who redeemed her when given get, breaking the chain

macro_segments.title: The Ran's Challenge to the Gemara's Logic: Woman as Subject, Not Object

micro_segments.title: Ran questions why woman's status as subject in kiddushin does not protect re-marriage
Ran argues both parties are participants in kiddushin, not just the man as actor
Lecturer explains Ran's view: Torah law treats woman as object of man's act, not equal actor
Historical context: from Torah's complete objectification to Chazal's requirement of consent to modern reconceptualization

macro_segments.title: Categories and Linguistic Meaning: Yordei Hayam (Seafarers) and Yoshvei Hayabashah (Land-dwellers)

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: vow against seafarers does not include short-distance travelers; only far-journey seafarers
Two readings: one limits 'seafarers' to far-journey travelers; other includes them in 'land-dwellers'
Explanation: both readings hinge on whether short-trip sailors are included in their respective categories

macro_segments.title: Non-literal Interpretation: Those Who See the Sun

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: vow against 'those who see the sun' includes blind people, not excluding them
Explanation: language is reinterpreted as 'those whom the sun sees' not 'those who see the sun'
Exclusion of fish and fetuses: creatures not visible to the sun

macro_segments.title: Generic Terms in Language: Black-headed (Shcharei HaRosh) as Men, Not Hair Color

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: vow against 'black-headed' excludes bald men, white-haired men, women, and children
Gemara explanation: term used to distinguish men from women and children, not literally about hair color
Reason: men's hair sometimes visible (uncovered), women's always covered, children's always uncovered
Tosafot discussion on whether minor girls' hair is covered or uncovered

macro_segments.title: Past vs. Future Birth: Yeludim and Noladim as Distinct or Overlapping Categories

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: vow against 'yeludim' (past-born) permits 'noladim' (future-born)
Mishnah continuation: vow against 'noladim' forbids both past and future births
Rabbi Meir's position: 'noladim' means only future-born, not past-born
Chachamim's position: 'noladim' encompasses both past and future births
Rav Pappa challenges Rabbi Meir using Torah usage of 'nolad' showing both past and future meaning
Resolution: in nedarim, lashon bnei adam (common usage) determines meaning, not lashon Torah
Chachamim: common usage tracks Torah usage for 'nolad'; Rabbi Meir: common usage differs
Exclusion: 'noladim' excludes fish and birds (hatched, not born)

topical_tags.term: Bar Padda's doctrine of continuous re-sanctification
Woman as object vs. subject in kiddushin
Lashon bnei adam (common usage) vs. lashon Torah (Torah usage)
Categories of people: yordei hayam (seafarers) and yoshvei hayabashah (land-dwellers)
Generic terms: shcharei harosh (black-headed) meaning men
Rabbi Meir vs. Chachamim on meaning of noladim
